text:
Plant-derived exosome-like nanovesicles (PDENs) have attracted much attention in the field of drug delivery and disease therapeutics. However, their standardized production is largely hampered by the unknown information about the mechanisms of their biogenesis, their molecular composition, and their specific marker proteins. Here, we, for the first time, isolated and characterized novel plant-derived chemotherapeutic immune modulators from the apoplastic fluid of Catharanthus roseus (L.) Don leaves (CLDENs). Quantitative analyses revealed that the membrane-bound CLDENs had a mean diameter of 75.51 ± 10.19 nm and a zeta potential of −21.8 mV. Moreover, the prepared CLDENs showed good stability under various conditions such as enzymolysis, pH and gastro fluids simulation.

The biodistribution of CLDENs revealed rapid uptake by immune cells and enrichment in immune-related organs following intraperitoneal injection. Lipidomic analysis of CLDENs revealed a distinct lipid molecular profile with an ether-phospholipid component making up 36.5% of its lipids. Differential proteomic analysis revealed that CLDENs are derived from multivesicular bodies (MVBs) in dendritic cells and we report for the first time the identification of six marker proteins associated with these unique organelles. Experiments using various concentrations of CLDENs (60-240 μg/mL) revealed that they had the ability to enhance the functions of macrophages, including promoting the classical alternative polarization of macrophages, enhancing their phagocytic capabilities, as well as promoting lymphocyte proliferation. In vivo treatment of mice with CLDENs at concentrations ranging from 20 to 60 mg/kg for three consecutive days not only restored the peripheral blood leukocyte number and overall cell counts in these animals to physiological ranges following cyclophosphamide-induced leukopenia, but also relieved the cycle arrest of haematopoietic cells in the bone marrow. Enhanced tumour-associated macrophage (TAM) production of tumour necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) was measured in CLDENs stimulated NF-κB signaling in primary cultured CD11b+ macrophages and enhanced expression of the haematopoietic transcription factor PU.1.

For industrial production, we established a plant cell culture system using Catharanthus roseus. This system yielded a three-fold higher amount of nanovesicles than that produced by conventional plant extraction method, amounting to several gram quantities. In summary, CLDENs were found to be stable, biocompatible nano-biomaterials that can act as effective immune adjuvants to improve post-chemotherapy patient recovery.
